caveats: a: BLS occupation data combine sawmills with wood preservation and cover employers rather than self-employed workers.
a: No national survey reports the installed base of AI-capable scanners by mill size.
rho: Vendor evidence demonstrates availability, not representative adoption or realized substitution.
rho: The frozen LMM band may include mills with sharply different automation economics.
e: Census reports 2,691 employer establishments, not firms or the frozen EBITDA-band population.
e: The injected n estimate is margin-bridged and may be especially unstable in a cyclical commodity industry.
s5: The retirement statistics are not national or sawmill-specific.
s5: Intent to retire is not an observed control-transfer rate and may end in closure.
q: Public-company commentary concerns larger North American producers, not the LMM population.
q: Retention differs between commodity softwood, specialty hardwood, custom grades, and by-products.
d5: Housing starts are not lumber consumption and the cited forecast covers only 2026-2027.
d5: The current demand base may be cyclically depressed, making ratios sensitive to the starting month.
o: Some demand can move to engineered wood, steel, concrete, imports, or vertically integrated producers.
o: This primitive concerns operator requirement, not labor intensity inside the operator.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat product sales to builders, distributors, dealers, industrial users, and secondary wood manufacturers, generally at market-linked prices rather than recurring service fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive mills, non-operating timber ownership, logging contractors, purchased-lumber millwork, veneer and engineered-panel production, and mills without a transferable operating business.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. sawmills producing dimension lumber, boards, beams, timbers, ties, shingles, shakes, siding, chips, and related planed output for repeat external buyers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader 321100 industry, where the largest occupations are physical production, material-moving, maintenance, and driving roles; industry automation evidence shows machine vision already addresses inspection, grading, sawing, edging, trimming, and sorting. I translate that task evidence into a wage-weighted not-yet-automated AI opportunity for 321113. The broader industry and omission of installed-base data could bias either way, while long-standing automation likely makes generic AI exposure overstate remaining opportunity.
caveats: BLS occupation data combine sawmills with wood preservation and cover employers rather than self-employed workers.
No national survey reports the installed base of AI-capable scanners by mill size.
a.change_evidence: A representative mill-level time-and-wage study separating manual from already automated grading, optimization, maintenance, scheduling, sales, and administration would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is dominated by physical plant roles, limiting software-only substitution. Remaining opportunity is concentrated in visual inspection and grading, process optimization, predictive maintenance, scheduling, documentation, quoting, and back-office work; mature scanner adoption means much of the obvious production opportunity is not new.

d5.bridge: NAHB forecasts single-family starts up 1% in 2026 and 5% in 2027, while multifamily starts decline in both years. I use housing starts as a major but incomplete volume proxy for constant-quality sawmill output demand, then extend cautiously across five years and allow repair/remodel, industrial, export, and substitution effects. Housing weakness may understate demand for specialty and industrial lumber and overstate demand where material substitution accelerates.
caveats: Housing starts are not lumber consumption and the cited forecast covers only 2026-2027.
The current demand base may be cyclically depressed, making ratios sensitive to the starting month.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year end-use forecast from Forest Economic Advisors or WWPA decomposing lumber consumption by new housing, repair/remodel, industrial, exports, and species/grade would materially improve the estimate.
d5.rationale: Baseline demand should remain near current levels with modest upside from eventual housing normalization, but affordability, rates, tariffs, construction cyclicality, engineered substitutes, and capacity rationalization create a wide range.

caveats: Census reports 2,691 employer establishments, not firms or the frozen EBITDA-band population.
The injected n estimate is margin-bridged and may be especially unstable in a cyclical commodity industry.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 496 frozen band candidates identifying captive ownership, external-customer revenue share, normalized EBITDA through a lumber cycle, and going-concern status would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Most sawmills sell output repeatedly to external customers and therefore fit the operating-business lens. Exclusions arise from captive facilities, very customer-concentrated or distressed mills, non-transferable owner-dependent operations, and records whose EBITDA bridge misclassifies commodity-cycle earnings.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 321113 ($4,883,678,264) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 321113 ($40,566,244,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $514,219 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 321113); class <5: 853 firms, avg revenue/firm $733,148 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $107,406 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 426 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,870,178 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $273,981 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 396 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,664,806 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $536,894 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 496 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,586,706 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,136,952 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 121 firms, avg revenue/firm $71,949,091 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,540,542 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 55 firms, avg revenue/firm $395,492,073 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $57,939,589 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 496

caveats: Some demand can move to engineered wood, steel, concrete, imports, or vertically integrated producers.
This primitive concerns operator requirement, not labor intensity inside the operator.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of autonomous lights-out mills operating across variable species and grades, or downstream customers vertically integrating conversion at scale, would lower this value.
o.rationale: Physical conversion of irregular logs into graded lumber still requires an accountable mill operator owning and maintaining industrial assets, managing safety and fiber procurement, certifying quality, and delivering output. AI can automate tasks inside the mill but is unlikely to replace the operating entity within five years.

caveats: Public-company commentary concerns larger North American producers, not the LMM population.
Retention differs between commodity softwood, specialty hardwood, custom grades, and by-products.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and invoice panels from independent mills showing benchmark resets, price-review frequency, bid win/loss behavior, and realized gross-margin persistence after documented automation projects would change the estimate.
q.rationale: Lumber is market-priced and volatile, so buyers can capture part of cost improvement through benchmark-linked pricing and competition. A mill can retain benefits from better recovery, grade yield, uptime, and throughput that are less visible than direct labor removal, but five-year repricing erodes a substantial share.

caveats: Vendor evidence demonstrates availability, not representative adoption or realized substitution.
The frozen LMM band may include mills with sharply different automation economics.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor installation cohorts reporting five-year adoption, commissioning time, false-reject rates, uptime, and realized labor-hour savings at independent U.S. mills below the large-enterprise tier would move this range.
rho.rationale: Commercial machine-vision and optimization systems are established in sawmill workflows, so technically tractable use cases can diffuse within five years. Brownfield integration, capital constraints, safety requirements, variable wood defects, sparse data at small mills, and operator acceptance prevent full implementation.

s5.bridge: An SBA sawmill-related transition case cites New York survey evidence that 57% of business owners wanted to retire within five years and fewer than one in five had a credible succession plan. I haircut retirement intent heavily because the source is regional and cross-industry and retirement does not necessarily produce a qualifying control sale; poor planning can cause closure or internal transfer. This likely overstates sale probability if retirement intent is treated as transaction flow.
caveats: The retirement statistics are not national or sawmill-specific.
Intent to retire is not an observed control-transfer rate and may end in closure.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, or Axial transaction records matched to NAICS 321113, plus owner-age and succession-plan responses from an Hardwood Manufacturers Association or regional forestry survey, would materially narrow the range.
s5.rationale: Rural, often closely held mills face genuine succession pressure, but capital intensity, cyclicality, environmental liabilities, customer concentration, and family or employee transitions reduce the share becoming qualifying external control transfers.

principal_driver: Physical lumber demand and established machine-vision use cases support durable, implementable operating improvements.
principal_weakness: Mature automation and commodity price competition limit incremental AI labor exposure and retained benefit.
